behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: The tool must correctly invoke git with rename-detection flags (e.g., git diff-index --diff-filter=R) and parse the output. A Unit test with mocked git output could pass while the real tool fails if: (1) the git command doesn't use the right flags, (2) git output format differs from expectations, or (3) git version differences affect output. Only a real git repo with an actual rename catches these integration gaps. MOCK-MISS applies: mocking subprocess.run() to return expected output doesn't verify the tool invokes the right git command.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repo with a committed file, rename it in a new commit, then run the CLI tool. Parse the output and verify: (1) exactly one entry in the 'renamed' category, (2) zero entries in both 'deleted' and 'added' for the renamed file, (3) the rename relationship is correctly reported (old→new path).

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Binary files trigger special git diff behavior (e.g., git outputs 'Binary files differ' or omits full diff). The tool must handle this output without crashing and include binary files in counts. A Unit test that mocks subprocess.run() could mock only the happy-path response, but real git's binary file output might trigger unexpected parsing logic or edge cases. Only a real git repo with committed binary files (e.g., .png, .zip, .pdf) tests against actual git output format and the tool's robustness.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repo with a committed text file and a binary file (e.g., PNG image). Modify the text file and add a binary file in a new commit. Run the CLI tool. Verify: (1) the tool exits cleanly (no crash), (2) the binary file appears in the 'added' count, (3) the modified text file is counted correctly, (4) the output structure is valid.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: An empty git repo (git init, no commits) triggers different git command behavior. Commands like 'git log' may error, return empty output, or behave unexpectedly on first initialization. A Unit test could mock git to return empty results, but the real failure would be: the tool runs a git command that actually fails on a new repo and doesn't catch the error gracefully. Only testing against a real empty repo verifies the tool handles the actual git behavior (e.g., no HEAD ref, no commits to iterate).
behaviors.test_description: Create a fresh git repo (git init) with zero commits. Run the CLI tool against it. Verify: (1) the tool exits with status 0 (success), (2) the output is a valid empty report (no errors, no stack trace), (3) all categories (added, deleted, modified, renamed) show zero counts or are omitted gracefully.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end correctness check that only real git repos can verify. The tool must correctly parse and count all changes (adds, deletes, modifies, renames) across the full commit history. Unit tests with mocked data would only verify that the tool processes mocked output; they don't verify the tool correctly interprets real git diffs or handles all change types. A real repo with known, auditable changes is the only way to verify counts are accurate.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repo with a known sequence of commits: (1) add 3 files, (2) modify 2 of them, (3) delete 1 file, (4) rename 1 file, (5) add 2 more files. Run the CLI tool and parse the report. Verify counts match exactly: added=5, modified=2, deleted=1, renamed=1. Repeat with different sequences to ensure correctness across varied commit histories.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: The tool may analyze different git refs or working-directory state depending on flags. Testing that staged vs unstaged changes produce the same output requires creating both states in a real git repo and running the tool on each. The tool might invoke different git commands (e.g., 'git diff --cached' vs 'git diff HEAD') or read different tree objects; only a real repo with actual staged and unstaged changes can verify the output equivalence. MOCK-MISS applies: mocking subprocess output wouldn't expose bugs in which git command the tool invokes or how it handles the staging area.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repo with an initial commit. Make a change to a file, stage it (git add). Make another change to the same file and leave it unstaged. Run the CLI tool on the repo and capture output A. Reset the repo to clean state, reapply both changes but do NOT stage them, then run the tool again and capture output B. Verify that outputs A and B are identical (same counts, same file lists, same report structure).
